title: Cat Bulging Eye (Exophthalmos): Emergency Triage & Symptoms Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has a bulging eye, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is critical to save their vision and eye health.
content_html:
What is a Bulging Eye in Cats?

A bulging eye, medically referred to as exophthalmos, occurs when the eyeball is pushed forward out of its natural position in the socket. This condition often indicates an underlying issue behind the eye, such as an abscess, inflammation, a tumor, or trauma. In some cases, the eyelid may not be able to close over the eye, leading to secondary dryness and damage to the cornea.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. A bulging eye is a high-priority veterinary emergency. It is typically very painful and poses an immediate threat to your cat's vision. Delaying treatment can lead to permanent blindness, corneal ulcers, or the surgical necessity to remove the eye (enucleation). If you notice your cat's eye appearing larger or protruding more than the other, contact an emergency clinic immediately.

How a Photo Can Help with Triage

Capturing a clear photo of your cat's eye from a few different angles can be incredibly helpful for a triage team. A photo allows a veterinarian to assess the degree of protrusion, check for pupil abnormalities, and look for signs of trauma or discharge before you even arrive at the clinic. This visual information helps the staff prepare the necessary equipment and prioritize your cat's care the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: What causes a cat's eye to bulge suddenly?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include retrobulbar abscesses (an infection behind the eye), head trauma, sudden glaucoma, or rapidly growing tumors in the eye socket.

faqs.question: Can I treat a bulging eye at home?
faqs.answer: No. Exophthalmos requires professional medical diagnosis and treatment, which may include draining an infection, reducing intraocular pressure, or emergency surgery. Do not attempt to push the eye back into the socket.

faqs.question: How do I transport my cat safely to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure, dark carrier to minimize stress. If the eye is protruding significantly and cannot close, you can gently cover it with a clean, water-moistened gauze pad to prevent the surface from drying out during the drive.
